pattern: When every unit you sell from a pile drops that pile's price by one, the most profitable strategy is to always sell from whichever pile currently sits highest, which turns into selling whole price 'tiers' across all piles tied at the top at once.
hints: Selling one ball from the tallest pile always beats selling from a shorter one, so sort the piles from tallest to shortest and think in terms of price levels rather than individual balls.

Process the piles from the top down: while a group of the tallest piles all share the same price, you can sell a whole horizontal 'slice' across all of them before the price has to drop further -- use the arithmetic-series sum for a full slice instead of simulating one ball at a time.

Once your remaining order budget can't cover a full slice down to the next tier, only part of that slice gets sold at the top price and the rest at one price lower; work out that split with plain division and remainder, then stop -- and remember the final total can be huge, so return it modulo 1,000,000,007.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, larger order count needing two tiers
single pile, sell every ball in it
many piles tied at the same height, forces a wide first tier
orders equal to the total balls available, must drain everything
one tall pile and several short ones, tests skipping past short piles
large single value with a modest order count

stress.description: random pile heights with random order counts within the total supply
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 5000))
    inventory = [rng.randint(1, 1000000) for _ in range(n)]
    total = sum(inventory)
    orders = rng.randint(1, total)
    return [inventory, orders]

stress.description: large inventory with huge per-pile values and a large order count
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    inventory = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    orders = rng.randint(1, 1000000000)
    return [inventory, orders]

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def maxProfit(self, inventory, orders):
        MOD = 10 ** 9 + 7
        inventory.sort(reverse=True)
        inventory.append(0)
        profit = 0
        width = 0
        n = len(inventory)
        for i in range(n - 1):
            width += 1
            cur = inventory[i]
            nxt = inventory[i + 1]
            if cur <= nxt:
                continue
            span = cur - nxt
            count = span * width
            if orders >= count:
                profit += (cur + nxt + 1) * span // 2 * width
                orders -= count
            else:
                q, r = divmod(orders, width)
                profit += (cur + cur - q + 1) * q // 2 * width
                profit += r * (cur - q)
                orders = 0
                break
        return profit % MOD
